@IdanAsherBlank @boknilev @yuvalpi However, how the language system exchanges information with other large-scale neural networks and what the precise nature and scope of such interactions are remain important questions for future research.

@IdanAsherBlank @boknilev @yuvalpi With respect to the latter, it is important to consider both the role of language in other cognitive abilities and the role of other cognitive abilities in language processing."

@ah__cl To my understanding, that is exactly what grounded cognition claims (Fillmore could be said to be a forebear of this through his influence on Lakoff). Those basic schemas (FORCE, MOTION, CONTAINMENT, etc) are learned through interactive, grounded experience.

Elman makes some interesting points about innateness and lexical concepts (https://t.co/n3agSp1Y5g).  While he gets a lot of NLP citations for his work on RNNs, his hypothesis that words' meanings lie in the causal effects they have on mental states seems far less explored. https://t.co/ZSO0nesBA5

The Poverty of Stimulus argument has a somewhat ironic re-interpretation illuminated by today's statistical language models- their "mental states" are poor substitutes for those gained by embodied agents operating in rich environments.

@alex_ander @barryjdevereux @neuranna @FelixHill84 @wzuidema @JoakimNivre @rensbod @tallinzen This also makes me wonder about learned hard attention models (e.g., https://t.co/PekfJGypnp) for reading which may have more flexibility in retrieving context (decoupling context retrieval from the number of layers).

@alex_ander @barryjdevereux @neuranna @FelixHill84 @wzuidema @JoakimNivre @rensbod @tallinzen Nick Chater refers to McConkie & Rayner's (1975) studies on attention during reading in his Mind is Flat book: https://t.co/oPcGrGlc00

@FelixHill84 @arumshisky @tallinzen @barryjdevereux @neuranna @wzuidema @JoakimNivre @rensbod @lena_voita See Elman (father of RNNs, https://t.co/n3agSp1Y5g): "Although language is clearly not mainly about prediction, there is good evidence that expectancy gener- ation plays a role in language comprehension (e.g. [21,22]). Prediction thus has psychological plausibility."

@FelixHill84 @arumshisky @tallinzen @barryjdevereux @neuranna @wzuidema @JoakimNivre @rensbod @lena_voita Meant to say that it seems next word prediction is an easy objective to optimize for, but far from the most significant one from a cognitive point of view.

@FelixHill84 @arumshisky @tallinzen @barryjdevereux @neuranna @wzuidema @JoakimNivre @rensbod @lena_voita I like an objective proposed by @glupyan : language programs the mind (https://t.co/PunGf9toO8), so using language is like programming the recipient's mind. Fleshing out this metaphor seems to lead to some interesting implications.
@FelixHill84 @le_roux_nicolas @kchonyc @tallinzen Agreed, we want "alignment of mental spaces" in some sense (https://t.co/HeUaROR9hB), only we don't know how to define those so well yet :(

@FelixHill84 @le_roux_nicolas @kchonyc @tallinzen "The evidence shows that communicators and addressees achieve mutual understanding by using the same computational procedures, implemented in the same neuronal substrate, and operating over temporal scales independent from the signals’ occurrences."

Blue-sky proposal paper accepted for spotlight @ Bridging AI and Cognitive Science workshop, ICLR2020! https://t.co/i0ZKePIa4n
Work with @GabiStanovsky  @rtsarfaty  and Dafna Shahaf (@HyadataLab)
1/n

As suggested by intriguing recent research such as @FelixHill84 , many desirable but elusive cognitive/NLU capabilities, such as systematic generalization and analogical reasoning, may need to be "grown" more than hard-coded:
2/n

emergent from interaction within rich environments rather than only elaborate neural architectures. we accordingly suggest (following extensive contemporary cog-sci) treating environments as first-class citizens in semantic representations, worthy of R&D in their own right.
3/n

We take some first steps towards this and outline a new semantic meaning representation based on interactive environments, which can be programmatically interacted with and created through text. 
4/n

We suggest that models must begin to understand and program in the language of affordances (which define possible actions in a given situation), as demonstrated for example by interactive fiction programming languages like Inform. 
5/n

Simple interactive Inform demos of text-based environments (more to follow!) at https://t.co/KBDennA5oy
6/n

Cool paper on neural connectivity patterns in creative individuals.
(1) default network generates ideas (using memory retrieval + mental simulation)
(2) salience network identifies potential candidates
(3) executive network elaborates promising ideas
https://t.co/8894SnsVG5

Notably, they suggest that creativity emerges from harmonious co-activation of the default and executive networks which normally work in opposition (either one or the other). 
It also reminds me of IR - first high recall step to find candidates, then high precision to filter
I couldn't exactly understand from the paper, but it seems to support the idea that right hemi plays central role in high creative individuals- seems like most of the hubs are located there (fig. 1)?

Really liked this work. Interesting take home maybe relevant also for NLP - learning to execute helps generalization, directly predicting answers doesn't.  In current GNN works, direct mappings are learned from raw inputs to solutions (e.g., predicting node reachability).
(1/n) https://t.co/WteOEBq5wJ

However, direct mapping approaches often take statistical shortcuts e.g, predict most nodes are reachable. But they won't learn reliable subroutines generalizable to trickier cases. Here they show providing stronger supervision in the form of intermediate execution steps (2/n)
(using execution trace of classic graph algos like BFS) is instrumental in training networks with stronger generalization capabilities (train on small graphs, generalize at test time to large graphs)
Forward thinking :) https://t.co/uvoAU1lEh7

Interesting framework for event cognition + comprehensive lit. review.  Human mental representations are predictive of real-world events- how are those different than  LM representations which are predictive of language often describing events? (1/n)
https://t.co/jCgWc0bfAH

Key properties of events: 1) composed of roles, which are central to structure of language. The thematic/semantic roles of events are largely present in language and are readily identifiable 2) convey state of change in the world this info. is largely absent from language. (2/n)

Makes me think of (1) being like parameters of api calls to library functions (2) shared between communicator and recipient. Reconstructing event semantics (2) based on (1) is possible only to a limited extent as discussed in Bender & Koller https://t.co/8tqg0jz2cD
(3/n)

"Woman swallows tea" implies (implicitly) state changes in tea and woman. Paper theorizes event comprehension enacted using hierarchical probabilistic forward world model (a. la Tennenbaum) which is activated across modalities (language, images, etc) (4/4)

to building the kinds of (internalized, differentiable) priors necessary for robust generalization in NLU and reasoning.

🧑‍🔬🧪We'll be presenting our work on process-level annotation+modelling of scientific procedural texts this week@ #EACL2021! Poster session 23/4 13-15 UTC, we'd love to chat :)
Project page: https://t.co/jGNH2kIWKt
Great team effort w/ @loadingfan, @alan_ritter, @GabiStanovsky! > https://t.co/cY8vI70nFQ

TL;DR: we used "text-based-gamification" to obtain rich, grounded annotations of procedural texts. 
Previous works focused mainly on text mining and have annotated only sentence-level shallow semantic structures. We're aiming for the setting of automated  execution, so >

we had annotators execute procedures in an interactive text-based game lab simulator, yielding Process Execution Graphs (PEGs). 
The grounding and gamification helped achieve good agreement on dense, complex scientific texts. PEGs capture interesting structures like >

branching workflows (first prepare solutions separately, then mix them) and long range relations (take the beaker from sentence 1 and heat it in sentence 10). 
We used graph prediction methods to predict PEGs from procedural texts- models performed well on >

local relation extraction and entity identification, while process level structure and long range relations are still quite challenging.
For the future, text-based game annotation opens up a novel direction of text-based RL methods for NLU on real world procedural text! 🧪🤖

@dan_s_becker @Thom_Wolf Shameless self-plug, but we tried to target exactly the regime of more abstract (supra-modal) mental simulation + text in our "blue-sky" paper from ACL2020 
https://t.co/TvCuUx76hB

@SergeThill @Thom_Wolf Agreed - following Lakoff and Barsalou, we also proposed that abstract concepts may not be so abstract after all. E.g., image schema (blue in figure below) are thought to play a role in conveying abstract ideas but are grounded in interaction.
https://t.co/rpIoa6Pc2p https://t.co/DQBgLuVrxQ

Excited to share our new preprint! We take a deep dive into the bAbI benchmark. Why bAbI? It targets a core NLU competency of *grounded situation modelling* for which very few benchmarks currently exist https://t.co/QDygVlKtG4. Longish 🧵> 1/ https://t.co/WIsC8e2KBz

Many models were (and continue to be) developed using bAbI, but other works have raised Qs about the reliability of the dataset and hence results achieved.  We show that bAbI still has a lot to offer, but that unlocking its potential requires showing some 💜 to the data side 2/

The main issue with bAbI is that the existing tasks are too easy, and there is no simple way to create more complex tasks. So we developed a new task generator which provides "dials" controlling task complexity. 🎛️ 3/

We created new test sets requiring compositional generalization, or transfer learning, across bAbI tasks. As the example shows, we check whether models trained on the original bAbI tasks could generalize to more complex stories involving novel combinations of concepts: 4/ https://t.co/yxHP24IdsJ

We tested both special-purpose models developed for bAbI as well as SOTA pre-trained methods, and found that while both approaches solve the original tasks (>99% accuracy), neither approach succeeded in the compositional generalization setting, 5/ https://t.co/fFPqwwhRcD

These results suggest limitations in the original training data, and also raise questions about the degree to which special purpose models developed for relation reasoning etc are fulfilling their intended design. 6/

We next explored ways to augment the original data, and found that though diversifying training data was far more useful than simply increasing dataset size, it was still insufficient for driving robust compositional generalization (with <70% accuracy for complex compositions) 7/

We think these results raise questions about the viability of learning robust situation models with standard QA training- maybe other training regimes or more structured representations are needed, similar to findings in https://t.co/3MK8UMb2Ep 8/

Finally, as a sanity check for the quality of our new tasks, we used the SQuAD concurrence metric introduced by @nelsonfliu  https://t.co/oJWtuSKsdH (how well a benchmark preserves the SQuAD rankings)- while the original study found that bAbI suffered from very low concurrence 9/
our new tasks exhibit high concurrence, in some cases as much as non-synthetic datasets: 10/ https://t.co/kMU18U0us1

Similar to sentiments raised in https://t.co/SeljJjJOBY , the story of bAbI seems to have predominantly been a case of "everyone wants to do model work"; R&D on tooling for simulators/data generation doesn't (yet!) get a lot of attention. 11/

We think our results highlight the usefulness of synthetic data, and also the importance of highly controllable task generators for facilitating "agile" model and data development.  12/ https://t.co/7ytf49JdYY

This project was quite a 🐇🕳️- I had expected to immediately work on the modelling side, it took a while to understand that the data side was a big part of the story. Great team effort with Kyle Richardson, Aviad Sar-Shalom,Noam Kahlon, @nelsonfliu @rtsarfaty  and @HyadataLab 13/

We're still working on getting the code ready for public release, but hope these results could already be of interest. Thanks for getting all the way to the end :)
And happy to hear any feedback! 14/14

For sure, these are impressive demonstrations, but I wish we’d be more careful using "wishful mnemonics" like “chains of thought” and “common-sense reasoning” (similar sentiments expressed by @MelMitchell1 ) > https://t.co/IUtO4Sq2sm

As described by @mpshanahan , at the core of common-sense are understanding of objects, motion, space, and causality (https://t.co/SPTIgyQ5Ye), which short & multi-choice benchmarks like CommonsenseQA do not rigorously evaluate.  >

Rigorous common sense eval should involve few shot compositional generalization tasks. 
https://t.co/W0Zrl2pVgp >

the bAbI tasks might be synthetic language, but at least the simulated microworld approach allows us to benchmark models on those core concepts (albeit a very small subset, but at least it’s a start) >

Here’s a toy example showing that, *in domain*, GPT3 can indeed answer babi questions and produce plausible explanations, given some prompts:
> https://t.co/PFOb3ba8aE

But it fails on a harder example requiring compositional generalization (we saw it "knows" the apple is in the park but can’t use that to infer that so is Mary)
> https://t.co/ar5YXD2oyt
Even providing a similar example in the prompts doesn’t seem to help.. https://t.co/tLZkjEryPS

1/Excited to share some new work on collective sensemaking! We’ll present it in the Blue Sky Ideas track (~open, exciting areas for R&D) of the upcoming @ACMHT This is a collective effort of @daostack, Veeo, @InferenceActive @CSenseMakers @HyadataLab https://t.co/JAcnB4YSpk🧵

@ACMHT @daostack @InferenceActive @CSenseMakers @HyadataLab 2/TL;DR: lots of talk about free speech online, but what about *free attention*, and why is it key for sensemaking? Free speech=freely creating new content; open-sourcing/decentralizing code, finance, social media.. freeing attention=freely sharing *reactions* (likes, bookmarks,) https://t.co/tJVWCnPDi5

@ACMHT @daostack @InferenceActive @CSenseMakers @HyadataLab 3/Sense-making refers to processes by which agents make sense of their environment, by organizing sense data until the environment is understood well enough to enable reasonable decisions. But collective sensemaking is in a bad state... https://t.co/8XL7BU6iqb https://t.co/mHzMS1oFWs

@ACMHT @daostack @InferenceActive @CSenseMakers @HyadataLab 4/Online environments are a hugely challenging mix of info overload + opaque algorithms: giant platforms (Google, Facebook, etc) have gained unprecedented control over the means to create, search and distribute information. But centralized platform control has resulted in >

@ACMHT @daostack @InferenceActive @CSenseMakers @HyadataLab 5/opaque information ecologies, where network data and algorithms are effectively hidden away from public/scientific/ethical oversight. Opaque platforms are involved in a host of deeply problematic social phenomena, as noted by many @HumaneTech_ @tristanharris  @jbakcoleman https://t.co/helohDCwEU

@ACMHT @daostack @InferenceActive @CSenseMakers @HyadataLab @HumaneTech_ @tristanharris @jbakcoleman 6/We draw upon the concept of stigmergy to illustrate how we quite literally *make* collective sense, and we leverage the stigmergic perspective on sensemaking to propose practical + radical steps forward for rehabilitating our polluted information environments

@ACMHT @daostack @InferenceActive @CSenseMakers @HyadataLab @HumaneTech_ @tristanharris @jbakcoleman 7/Stigmergy refers to the phenomena of indirect coordination mediated by modifications of the environment, eg ant pheromone trails. The crazy part is that the env acts as a kind of distributed memory system for a collective organism:  https://t.co/HOrnJNF217 https://t.co/s289YsYM94

@ACMHT @daostack @InferenceActive @CSenseMakers @HyadataLab @HumaneTech_ @tristanharris @jbakcoleman 8/Agents modify the env, which affects same/other agents, constituting a cybernetic feedback loop. Pretty much all large scale coordination must rely on stigmergy (including for humans), so we really want to create healthy and well functioning stigmergic infrastructure! https://t.co/ORXbYJtH8x

@ACMHT @daostack @InferenceActive @CSenseMakers @HyadataLab @HumaneTech_ @tristanharris @jbakcoleman 9/Ant pheromone is an example of a particularly important type of env. modification known as stigmergic markers. Examples of markers in online spaces (“digital pheromone”)  are likes, hyperlinks, click-through data, read time, etc. 
https://t.co/KcpC9tsrED

@ACMHT @daostack @InferenceActive @CSenseMakers @HyadataLab @HumaneTech_ @tristanharris @jbakcoleman 10/Markers are signals that carry a lot of information yet are cheap to create and interpret, both by humans and machines, which explains why they are so precious to platforms running lucrative attention markets. 
See also excellent https://t.co/G5iHsqHwUS by @philipplenz6 et al

@ACMHT @daostack @InferenceActive @CSenseMakers @HyadataLab @HumaneTech_ @tristanharris @jbakcoleman @philipplenz6 11/Markers are in many ways digital traces of human attention! So what's our proposal? Stated simply, akin to the open source software (OSS) movement to free software, we propose an Open Source Attention (OSA) movement to free stigmergic marker data (~attention) from platforms >

@ACMHT @daostack @InferenceActive @CSenseMakers @HyadataLab @HumaneTech_ @tristanharris @jbakcoleman @philipplenz6 12/through a decentralized eco-system for creating, storing and querying stigmergic markers. OSA involves both social and tech shifts: the social side involves developing a “sense-maker" ethos similar to the hacker/maker ethos around OSS eg @mbauwens https://t.co/8f6UTtgQ8L

@ACMHT @daostack @InferenceActive @CSenseMakers @HyadataLab @HumaneTech_ @tristanharris @jbakcoleman @philipplenz6 @mbauwens 13/OSA is similar to "open source thinking"/digital gardening, but doesn't even require writing new content. Sense-Makers are expert curators who share and are mindful of their attention (stigmergic traces) - they annotate, link, like, tag (etc) in public https://t.co/iNhFaQm9NF

@ACMHT @daostack @InferenceActive @CSenseMakers @HyadataLab @HumaneTech_ @tristanharris @jbakcoleman @philipplenz6 @mbauwens 14/On the tech side we envision ecologies empowering makers to create and share their stigmergic markers, with self-sovereign storage (@project_solid, @Filecoin, etc). Decoupling data and algos incentivizes maker-centered rather than user-exploiting algos (@RubenVerborgh) https://t.co/PJUDp8VlDS

@ACMHT @daostack @InferenceActive @CSenseMakers @HyadataLab @HumaneTech_ @tristanharris @jbakcoleman @philipplenz6 @mbauwens @project_solid @Filecoin @RubenVerborgh 15/Our proposal is congruent with the dweb/web3 “protocols, not platforms” approach (@mmasnick,@protocollabs,) - rather than competition between existing Personal Knowledge Platforms (PKPs) like @obsdmd @memexgarden @logseq @AthensResearch ,> https://t.co/OXW05TLKup

@ACMHT @daostack @InferenceActive @CSenseMakers @HyadataLab @HumaneTech_ @tristanharris @jbakcoleman @philipplenz6 @mbauwens @project_solid @Filecoin @RubenVerborgh @mmasnick @protocollabs @obsdmd @memexgarden @logseq @AthensResearch 16/ interoperable protocols/storage for stigmergic primitives can enable bootstrapping existing PKP growth for *collective* sensemaking/knowledge. https://t.co/rEU1til8tF

@ACMHT @daostack @InferenceActive @CSenseMakers @HyadataLab @HumaneTech_ @tristanharris @jbakcoleman @philipplenz6 @mbauwens @project_solid @Filecoin @RubenVerborgh @mmasnick @protocollabs @obsdmd @memexgarden @logseq @AthensResearch 17/There are still of course many open questions remaining; our short work (though long thread 😅) is primarly a “call to attention” to the pressing challenge of collectively learning to govern our attention

@ACMHT @daostack @InferenceActive @CSenseMakers @HyadataLab @HumaneTech_ @tristanharris @jbakcoleman @philipplenz6 @mbauwens @project_solid @Filecoin @RubenVerborgh @mmasnick @protocollabs @obsdmd @memexgarden @logseq @AthensResearch 18/As @WilliamsJames_ writes eloquently in https://t.co/Pvmm1zlIde, surmounting the formidable challenges faced today by humanity requires that “we give the right sort of attention to the right sort of things”. We hope our work can help galvanize action towards this vital cause!

@ACMHT @daostack @InferenceActive @CSenseMakers @HyadataLab @HumaneTech_ @tristanharris @jbakcoleman @philipplenz6 @mbauwens @project_solid @Filecoin @RubenVerborgh @mmasnick @protocollabs @obsdmd @memexgarden @logseq @AthensResearch @WilliamsJames_ 19/This initiative grew out of the wonderful, hopeful real-world and online spaces cultivated by @metagov and @RadxChange. Thanks for helping make this happen! 🙏🌱

@ACMHT @daostack @InferenceActive @CSenseMakers @HyadataLab @HumaneTech_ @tristanharris @jbakcoleman @philipplenz6 @mbauwens @project_solid @Filecoin @RubenVerborgh @mmasnick @protocollabs @obsdmd @memexgarden @logseq @AthensResearch @WilliamsJames_ @metagov @RadxChange 20/And thank you for your attention if you made it all the way here :) This is just the start of our journey and we'd be happy to connect with fellow sensemakers! To be in touch, follow us at @CSenseMakers or drop us a DM to join our collective! /end

RT @CSenseMakers: I am verifying my ownership of Open Source Attention on Gitcoin Grants at https://t.co/e434aQhlUf. 🔥🎈👍🙌🔥🎁

1/Check out our 1st @gitcoin grant at https://t.co/QsmA0N3x3c : a joint project of @CSenseMakers, @braddegraf, @lovevolv As we explored here, open sharing of attention signals (aka markers/annotations), eg bookmarks, tags, likes, is key to healthier information environments🧵 https://t.co/O0RmCBcDla

2/We're aiming to open-source social attention data by bootstrapping an open-access, platform-agnostic annotation corpus from existing behavior on social media, with an initial focus on scientific research.

3/Why? Attention signals are a form of stigmergy, a foundational coordination mechanism where individual signalling (such as pheromone trails in ants) drives emergent collective intelligence. Annotations online can function as a kind of "digital pheromone", but to unlock

4/ their true stigmergic potential and not lose them in the noise, annotations must be embedded within powerful content discovery services which leverage annos along with social signals (likes, follows etc) to surface & recommend relevant content. Most annotation platforms lack

5/ the social discoverability aspect (eg you can't like an anno on https://t.co/ZwdmPWogFS), while others include social information but don't provide access to it. Twitter is an amazing example of stigmergic annotation- likes, social graph etc drive high quality recommendations https://t.co/FzJfi0hEAv

6/ but Twitter also imposes lots of limitations, eg siloing data & constraining us to an ephemeral feed with opaque recommendation algos that we're unable to modify. We're hoping our initiative can be the start of a new protocol for sharing annotations from diverse sources

7/ such as @_Glasp, @hypothes_is, etc. Stigmergic annotations can then drive powerful collective sensemaking applications like epistemic "navigation trails" , layered reading experiences & personalized feeds. DeSci in particular can benefit from annotation sharing- https://t.co/nFfIc1B4pf

8/ scientific progress consists of layers of insights, but today these still are obfuscated by static pdfs and disparate threads of conversations siloed across a multitude of platforms. Many annotation sharing proposals exist, such as @AdamMarblestone's prescient Beagle project https://t.co/RO2lTNyFfs

9/ By building upon the amazing #web3 efforts on distributed storage, decentralized organization and human-centered incentives, we can bring these important visions closer to reality, and with them, more radically open and transformative science ⚛️🧪🌱 #desci #OpenScience

RT @ai2_aristo: 🎉Happy to share our @emnlp2022 paper! Ever wish your model had breakpoints you could inspect to probe its intermediate sema…

RT @oren_sultan: Can we teach NLU models to track their beliefs through intermediate points in the text? 🧐
Inspired by breakpoints in progr…

1/For the longer version 🧵: Breakpoints in programming are important tools for code interpretability as they allow inspection of program state at intermediate points during execution. We developed Breakpoint Transformers (BPTs) employing  “natural language breakpoints”  > https://t.co/iP081TPSe0

2/that can be used to probe encodings of input texts. Breakpoints are simply special tokens ([B]) inserted after each sentence. Breakpoint encoding vectors b can then be queried against a natural language proposition encoding p to obtain a {T,F,?} prediction for each b,p pair https://t.co/BARy8yTD3M

3/breakpoints are helpful for "debugging"- understanding model behavior and anticipating potential failure modes; e.g., to what degree do models exhibit commonsense world knowledge, for example that objects likely persist in their location unless the text mentions otherwise?

4/Beyond debugging LLMs, breakpoints are also useful for modelling procedural/narrative texts which require state tracking of entities across time; a single forward pass produces joint predictions for all states (rather than standard QA which is limited to a single relation)

5/The natural language format makes breakpoint modeling easily applicable to a wide variety of tasks. We experimented with bAbI (story understanding), CLUTRR (relational reasoning) and TRIP (physical commonsense reasoning) https://t.co/Gz9N5QMUCB

6/For CLUTRR, we showed that BPTs improved model prediction consistency (eg not predicting contradicting relations). For bAbI we showed that BPTs can accurately jointly predict hundreds of relations across long stories (20 sentences x ~15 propositions per sentence)

7/On TRIP we achieved 20-30% absolute improvement over the method introduced in the original paper, which employed a RoBERTA based architecture tailored specifically to TRIP (while BPT required no additional adaptation for this task)
8/breakpoints are like a modular plug-in: in all these tasks we added breakpoint modeling to existing QA Transformer models (T5) without harming the original QA performance.

9/We took a lot of inspiration from this great work of @belindazli which found that LLMs partially encode semantic information in entity mentions as a pretraining side-effect; we explicitly trained models to localize information to predefined encodings (breakpoints) https://t.co/IT964QPXcw

10/There is still a lot more work left! We found that breakpoint encodings inherit many of the same systematic generalization limitations of LLMs - encoding intermediate state information in this way did not markedly improve generalization performance

11/Also, while breakpoints provide an indication of intermediate model “beliefs'', they are also different from beliefs in important ways. In particular, the causal relation between information represented in breakpoints and generated model outputs is unclear

12/Despite these limitations, we expect that training models to correctly form intermediate beliefs and reason over them will be an important step towards more interpretable, robust and reliable NLU systems!
13/ Thanks again to all the team: Kyle Richardson, @oren_sultan,  @rtsarfaty , Dafna Shahaf ( @HyadataLab ) and Ashish Sabharwal! Code is at https://t.co/SoLxjHY87X . Thanks for reading!

1/Really enjoyed this talk - an insightful political philosophy perspective on how can we better govern collective attention in the age of global scale digital platforms (Facebook, Twitter, etc) dominating the public sphere. https://t.co/kADlmFP5Wd

2/We @csensemakers have come at this exact question from a collective intelligence perspective!
https://t.co/bkosq1NEpo
As @sethlazar notes, until now we have mostly been focused on protecting freedom of speech. But in a hyper-connected,  info rich world, the new game

@CSenseMakers @sethlazar 3/we must urgently understand is how to protect freedom of attention. Free speech is nearly meaningless when centralized platforms control who will attend to what you say. Yet neither states nor platforms have the right tools/incentives to govern attention. https://t.co/DSbarwKB7A

@CSenseMakers @sethlazar 4/In our work we offer another governance model challenging platforms’ control of our attention - platforms control our attention since they control our *digital markers of attention* (likes, votes, etc). https://t.co/O0RmCBcDla

5/Tech exists (self sovereign storage, decentralized apps, like @project_solid, @ceramicnetwork etc) to let us begin regaining control of our attention by regaining control of our digital attention traces. https://t.co/cst6YJ6rHn

@project_solid @ceramicnetwork 6/Open Source Attention - sharing these traces publicly (instead of donating them to platforms/ad-tech) can power a pluralistic and diverse recommendation system ecosystem, similarly to open source software’s immense contribution to a pluralistic software ecosystem. https://t.co/N7BygCbT6c

@project_solid @ceramicnetwork 7/This could be thought of as an Elinor Ostrom model of attention governance - individuals and communities empowered to self-govern + share their attention (curating reading lists, feeds, etc) in a polycentric infosphere  @quinndupont https://t.co/bQPE8MwR4h

@project_solid @ceramicnetwork @quinndupont 8/seeds of this can already be found in many decentralized orgs, eg @Neighbour_hoods. We’re also experimenting with a community curation system with @tecmns https://t.co/42ZINnNLew

@project_solid @ceramicnetwork @quinndupont @Neighbour_hoods @tecmns 9/"Self-infrastructuring" is a powerful lens on decentralized tech that can naturally also include infrastructure for governing attention  @kelsiemvn https://t.co/jH7YFc0NW9

@project_solid @ceramicnetwork @quinndupont @Neighbour_hoods @tecmns @kelsiemvn 10/Finally, beyond Ostrom, this kind of self-organizing also has deep connections to theories of collective intelligence (eg @VERSESAI) , allowing us to envision a very different kind of human-AI synthesis than the current exploitative, centralized corporate black box offerings > https://t.co/TymCUSlLWr

11/11 collective attention governance seems a whole new transdisciplinary research area, it would be cool to start a workshop or other gathering place for interested researchers and practitioners! @ntnsndr @pujaohlhaver @divyasiddarth

1/There is a follow up to this (excellent!) piece waiting to be written about LLMs as “hostile epistemic environments”, or “epistemic junk food”. I don’t have time for that yet, so meanwhile just a few thoughts 🧵 > https://t.co/37LJZeRQ1k

2/ Hostile epistemic envs exploit our cognitive vulnerabilities and weaknesses, which are plentiful in an age where information overload is stretching cognition beyond its limits. https://t.co/kFb9Zn8rX5
3/I have a hunch that LLMs, like conspiracy theories, work because they offer an intense dose of clarity/coherence, particularly valuable in an overwhelmingly complex world. https://t.co/Kpd13CjrMB

4/Hostile epistemic envs share important similarities with hostile nutritional envs (junk food): the companies creating them may not be intentionally trying to harm us, they want $$ and harm is a side effect. https://t.co/309l7RZTX5
5/Creators of epistemic junk food will ofc say- “but we’re helping people”! A good heuristic might be - helping in the short or long term?

6/ Loneliness chatbots may alleviate loneliness like cigarettes relieve stress, but have negative side effects (which corporations avoid accountability for), and so these magic LLMs apps should be viewed with our critical  thinking cap on tight.

7/This isn’t to say that there aren’t any non-“junk-food” uses of LLMs - there are many useful applications of the technology. But the “fairtrade/sustainable” uses are still rare comparatively to the junk uses, and those less exploitative uses are harder to turn a profit with.

8/Despite the not-quite-intentional hostility of corporations, greed can make you do pretty awful stuff: today we might shake our heads in disbelief that in the 50s, corporations convinced  ppl that 7up for babies was not only ok, it was even wholesome. https://t.co/uEQXEQTbRI

9/I hope that a few decades from now we’ll look back similarly at junk LLM products. However, with capitalism reaching deeper and deeper into our psyche in search of new territory to exploit, what will be left of us in a few decades is anyone’s guess 🤷 https://t.co/cvEamJbcSS

I keep coming back to this thread as I think it hints at an intriguing insight for AI research. Imagine you were in a world without Stack Overflow, and that you wanted to build an AI model to answer questions about programming.  1/ https://t.co/HFYqWGBJRS

We AI researchers would spend years thinking up fancy new modelling approaches, neuro-symbolic reasoning modules, graph neural networks etc. With hindsight though, we can see that it turns out that the most critical move would be to *build a platform like Stack Overflow*. 2/

That platform would collect the quality human interactions at the scale necessary for training a model. The challenges involved in building that platform would not be AI challenges at all, but rather human challenges; incentives, UX, moderation, etc. 3/
How many other so called “AI problems” that we’re trying to solve with fancy new modelling approaches, are actually more about building the right human interaction environments? 4/4

A lot has been written about how the traditional science model of publishing and peer review is broken. One connection I haven’t seen made is with the **sensemaking** literature, which studies how people understand complex topics 🧵  1/ https://t.co/Vr3dK4XkKK

Sensemaking is a powerful  framework that has been used mainly for analyzing organizations and surprisingly less for scientific research. But sensemaking really helps to see why the current traditional science model is broken, and also how we can improve things. 2/

While not directly mentioned in the figure, classical publishing and peer review are at the end stages of a nested, iterative multi-scale feedback loop. Information search, filtering, relating  concepts, etc are all part of the larger sensemaking process. 3/ https://t.co/Akw4PWDyOx

The sensemaking model shows us 2 important missing elements from trad-sci.
1) Publishing happens across diverse scales and formats, as demonstrated by the rapid rise of various micro/nanopublishing/discourse graph initiatives (@nanopub_org @Micropub7n @JoelChan86 ) 4/ https://t.co/aLGMpZKt62
For example, nanopublishing aims at the "smallest units of publishable information"; even someone declaring they’ve read a paper can be a nanopub!  5/ https://t.co/FoHsrg1Syc

2) Feedback also happens across diverse scales and formats. This is why scientists spend so much time on social media- we don't want to wait months for noisy peer reviews. Scientists posting on Twitter are actually nanopublishing but don’t know it yet! 6/ https://t.co/w3iwqEaJcN
That is a current blind spot in our scientific infrastructure! Social media networks are not open or FAIR, so are ill suited to host these “proto” nanopubs.  7/ https://t.co/Cbazds6aVA
The sensemaking framework suggests that for micro/nanopub efforts to succeed, they’ll need to be embedded in effective social feedback networks. 8/

The idea of social networks for scientists as been around for a long time, but with social media going FUBAR, IMO it’s high time to revisit these ideas and integrate them with the exciting progress on micro/nanopublishing 9/ https://t.co/DmF05hnZtT

I recently presented these ideas at @OSFramework MetaScience 2023 based on work with @InferenceActive  @CSenseMakers  - check out https://t.co/LpIyO30GEE for paper/slides/etc ! Still work in progress, and we'd love to engage with fellow sensemakers interested in this space!
10/10

New year, new ways to do science! 🍾🔭 I'm excited to share some details about Sensemaking Networks, the project I’ll be developing this year @AsteraInstitute! 🧵1/
Blog post version (2 parts): 
https://t.co/luSwcHXS1k
https://t.co/eZ0ysMt7mJ https://t.co/ceLVkDRnbQ

@AsteraInstitute Sensemaking Networks is taking up the question alluded to in this post. What if Twitter (and science social media more broadly) *was* real academic work? What would such networks look like if designed from first principles with the aim of supporting science? 2/ https://t.co/f0HCLrnV8N

@AsteraInstitute But first - why do we even need to change the ways we communicate science? Well, existing processes leave a lot to be desired, to say it lightly.. Specifically, we’re aiming to address three interrelated limitations of current science communication: 3/ https://t.co/V9pV8hPqoV

@AsteraInstitute (1) Lack of support for diverse publishing formats and scales. Why are we still just using papers? 🤔 We have much more exciting ways of sharing knowledge, but science has been slow in adopting them. 4/ https://t.co/m4JjW1aVwf

@AsteraInstitute Nanopublications @nanopub_org are a great example. A nanopub is like a social media post plus semantics, meaning that it’s actually a small knowledge graph piece! Think of them as “meaning legos” we can use to interconnect and contextualize information 🟥-🟦-🟨 5/ https://t.co/GjlGPM6FOi

@AsteraInstitute @nanopub_org Semantics may sound archaic, but semantic publishing is a truly radical idea since it empowers *us* to control how we relate to online content, instead of outsourcing that ability to centralized  platforms who often don't have our best interests in mind. 6/ https://t.co/Nkuf7znOla

@AsteraInstitute @nanopub_org (2) Feedback loops are too long, and reach is poor. In contrast, science social media can be brilliant for these: its public nature invites broad participation, and algorithmic feeds greatly improve reach: retweets by “science influencers” can lead to millions of views. 7/

@AsteraInstitute @nanopub_org (3) Non-traditional publications are siloed and fragmented. Sure, Open Science has significantly improved access to *traditional* papers, but when we consider science social media posts as such non-traditional pubs, we have a wild west of non-interoperating apps and formats. 8/

@AsteraInstitute @nanopub_org Platforms like Twitter enclose data as part of their business model, directly hindering open science. Even on open social platforms (eg Mastodon), lack of standards and support for semantic publishing makes it difficult to effectively aggregate, sort and index data at scale 9/ https://t.co/OCX9gsC5PW

@AsteraInstitute @nanopub_org A key insight key insight we'll be returning to throughout is that *researchers are already nanopublishing on social media, but just don’t know it yet*, and lack the infrastructure to do so more effectively. Sensemaking is a missing layer from the scientific record! 10/ https://t.co/aztWvNT089

@AsteraInstitute @nanopub_org Enter Sensemaking Networks! We envision Sensemaking Networks as decentralized social networks where posts are nanopublications, and data is open and FAIR. 11/ https://t.co/yOSslQYcwf

@AsteraInstitute @nanopub_org As shown in the Venn diagram, our approach aims to draw on the best of social media networks in terms of UX, feedback and reach, while addressing their limitations of fragmented data and restricted publishing expressivity. 12/ https://t.co/k6WhFJZD9c

@AsteraInstitute @nanopub_org Why are we using the term 'Sensemaking'?  Sensemaking captures the full research lifecycle, from the messy parts where knowledge hasn’t yet crystallized (these are valuable but ignored in the current system), until the finished "camera-ready" paper 👇 13/ https://t.co/BHRKJJfSG6
@AsteraInstitute @nanopub_org What’s our plan for building Sensemaking Networks? (expect ongoing iterations as we start building 👷🔄) 14/

@AsteraInstitute @nanopub_org (1) using NLP to convert science social media posts to nanopublications. Semantics + radical interoperability means nanopublications can power many downstream services -> 15/ https://t.co/Gg9SMNpCys

@AsteraInstitute @nanopub_org For example, consider aggregations of research mentions on social media, like on preprint servers such as bioRxiv. Semantics provide much more useful context compared with existing simple mention counters. Or you could build research recommender feeds...The sky is the limit! 16/ https://t.co/q2Cq1QkKzF

@AsteraInstitute @nanopub_org (2) Building a social nanopublishing app. Imagine Mastodon or Bluesky, but instead of publishing just posts, you could nanopublish, e.g., select a template best expressing the semantics of your message. 17/ https://t.co/o2Kh1nDvOF

@AsteraInstitute @nanopub_org A researcher's nanopublished assessment of some paper could then directly appear in context  that paper, in addition to appearing in social media feeds. Social media + semantic nanopublishing to realize the dream of a social annotation layer for the web! 18/ https://t.co/FzJfi0hEAv

@AsteraInstitute @nanopub_org Semantic publishing needs to be as easy and natural as posting on existing apps, and great UX + AI assistants will be key to making that possible! Friction + complexity were reasons that early semantic web projects didn't take off- we need to learn from those attempts. 19/

@AsteraInstitute @nanopub_org Different from services that track researchers unknowingly, we’re committed to consentful tech and participatory data practices. We see data sharing and collection as intentional practices employed by communities, for communities, to support collective sensemaking 20/ https://t.co/O0RmCBcDla

@AsteraInstitute @nanopub_org Our goal is not to build yet another social media platform! 🏰 Rather, open + collaborative ecosystems are both the means and ends of the project. That’s why we're building partnerships with mission-aligned orgs to evolve this new ecosystem, together 🤝 21/

Some updates on Sensemaking Networks! (blog version https://t.co/un9BhoaDbz) We built our first prototype for an LLM-augmented semantic cross-poster that can nanopublish @nanopub_org alongside posting to social media! LLMs suggest keywords + the nanopub semantic structure   1/ 🧵 https://t.co/8BB0lVjWmD

@nanopub_org 2/ The resulting nanopub is part of the Nanopublications network which can then be queried using the powerful SPARQL query language. For example, we can do reverse lookups to see all the nanopubs referencing a particular paper (click the link to try!) https://t.co/bjm2GrTFkl https://t.co/xa9ZEBzz4J
@nanopub_org 3/ we’re excited by the potential of LLM + knowledge graphs for next generation natural language interfaces that used to be firmly in the realm sci fi. https://t.co/CCpIInhrwQ

@nanopub_org 4/Note that these kinds of queries are *simply not possible even with the best LLMs today*, since they require KGs in addition to powerful NLP capabilities h/t to SPARQL wizard @ShaRefOh 🔮 https://t.co/Uhi56MYTwf
@nanopub_org @ShaRefOh 5/@pepoospina improved the nanopub private key management using web3 wallet services. You can now nanopublish using Metamask and your eth ID! https://t.co/px7i8H3L29
@nanopub_org @ShaRefOh @pepoospina 6/ We had our first IRL team meeting in DeSci Denver! https://t.co/u4BdtU0VCC

@nanopub_org @ShaRefOh @pepoospina 7/ @descieth was rad 🔥 We were especially energized by discussions around coordinated efforts to make de/open sci more mainstream.
